Equipment category.definition: Which equipment type you're evaluating (MRI, CT, Cath Lab, Dialysis, Ultrasound, Custom).
Equipment category.direction: Context-dependent — determines every other default, not itself a "higher/lower is better" value.
Equipment category.howToEstimate: Select the type matching your actual purchase.
Equipment category.whyItMatters: Drives every benchmark default shown elsewhere in the tool.

Equipment name / model (optional).definition: A free-text label for your own reference in the exported report.
Equipment name / model (optional).howToEstimate: Enter your vendor's model name if you want it on the report.
Equipment name / model (optional).whyItMatters: Cosmetic only; helps you tell exported reports apart later.

Hospital bed size.direction: Context-dependent — used as a lookup key for scale-dependent benchmarks, not a value with a "better" direction.
Hospital bed size.confidence: N/A (your own hospital's fact, not a benchmark).
Hospital bed size.howToEstimate: Use your hospital's registered/licensed bed count.
Hospital bed size.whyItMatters: A 50-bed and a 500-bed hospital see different typical utilization and vendor pricing; this is what lets the tool distinguish them.

City / tier.definition: Which city or city-tier (Tier 1/2/3) the hospital is in.
City / tier.howToEstimate: Select your actual city or nearest tier classification.
City / tier.whyItMatters: Feeds city-tier-dependent benchmarks (utilization, tariff) where research supports the breakdown.

Purchase cost.definition: The equipment's upfront price, before installation.
Purchase cost.direction: Lower is better — reduces upfront capital, shortens payback, improves NPV/IRR.
Purchase cost.defaultValue: See the selected equipment's `equipment-data/<type>.json#purchaseCost` — a wide range (configuration and brand vary enormously).
Purchase cost.confidence: Varies by equipment type — see the data file.
Purchase cost.howToEstimate: Use an actual vendor quotation wherever possible; benchmark ranges here are directional only.
Purchase cost.whyItMatters: The base of your initial investment and of depreciation.

Installation / civil cost.definition: One-time site preparation, civil work, and commissioning cost on top of the equipment's sticker price.
Installation / civil cost.defaultValue: Often expressed as a % of purchase cost — see `equipment-data/<type>.json#installationAndAncillaryCostPercentage`.
Installation / civil cost.confidence: Varies by equipment type — mostly Medium/Low, tender-mandated bid-allocation floors rather than measured actual spend.
Installation / civil cost.howToEstimate: Check whether your vendor's turnkey quote already includes this before entering it separately — double-counting here is a common mistake.
Installation / civil cost.whyItMatters: Adds directly to initial investment alongside purchase cost.

Expected months before revenue starts.definition: The gap between paying for the equipment and it seeing its first patient.
Expected months before revenue starts.direction: Lower is better — shorter delay means less pre-operative interest and an earlier start to revenue.
Expected months before revenue starts.confidence: Varies by equipment type; Dialysis/Ultrasound are Low confidence (informal/adjacent sources only).
Expected months before revenue starts.howToEstimate: Sum your own civil work, installation, licensing, and training timelines if you have them — see the Advanced Mode launch-delay breakdown for the individual components.
Expected months before revenue starts.whyItMatters: Longer delays mean more pre-operative interest accrues (if financed) before revenue offsets it.

Expected usage per day.direction: Higher is better, up to realistic capacity — the single largest lever on revenue and on clearing break-even.
Expected usage per day.confidence: Varies by equipment type; several are Medium or single-study-sourced — check the specific figure's confidence before relying on it.
Expected usage per day.howToEstimate: Prefer your own market/referral estimate over the benchmark if you have one; this is one of the weaker-researched fields overall.
Expected usage per day.whyItMatters: Drives both revenue and whether you clear break-even usage per day.

Average billed revenue per use.definition: The full invoiced/sticker price per scan or procedure, before payer deductions.
Average billed revenue per use.direction: Higher is better, within what the local market will bear.
Average billed revenue per use.confidence: Varies by equipment type and rate tier (NABH vs. non-NABH) — see the data file.
Average billed revenue per use.howToEstimate: Use your own tariff sheet; government-scheme rates (CGHS/PM-JAY) shown as benchmarks are a reimbursement floor, not your private cash tariff.
Average billed revenue per use.whyItMatters: The starting point for both billed and (after realization %) realized revenue.

Working days per month.definition: How many days per month the machine actually runs.
Working days per month.direction: Higher is better, within realistic operating limits.
Working days per month.defaultValue: 25 days/month, flat across all equipment types.
Working days per month.confidence: This is a modeling convention, not a researched benchmark — not calendar-accurate (doesn't vary 26/28/26 by month).
Working days per month.howToEstimate: Adjust if your facility has unusual closure patterns (e.g. more or fewer weekly closures than typical).
Working days per month.whyItMatters: A direct multiplier on both monthly revenue and monthly variable cost.

Consumable cost per use.definition: Per-use cost of contrast agent, dialyzer, gel, drapes, or whatever consumable applies to this equipment type.
Consumable cost per use.defaultValue: No centralized benchmark yet — vendor/consumable-contract dependent.
Consumable cost per use.howToEstimate: Use your actual consumable supply contract or recent invoices.
Consumable cost per use.whyItMatters: Part of variable cost per use, which sets your contribution margin per scan.

Professional / reporting fee per use.definition: The doctor's own fee for performing or reporting the procedure.
Professional / reporting fee per use.direction: Lower is better (from a margin perspective) but is a real, necessary cost, not one to minimize by omission.
Professional / reporting fee per use.defaultValue: No centralized benchmark yet — varies widely by specialty and market.
Professional / reporting fee per use.howToEstimate: Use your actual radiologist/cardiologist/sonologist fee arrangement.
Professional / reporting fee per use.whyItMatters: Kept in Basic Mode deliberately, not buried in Advanced Mode — omitting it can make break-even look falsely optimistic. Distinct from a separate referral/commission arrangement, which this tool doesn't model (`ISSUES.md` ISS-11).

Other variable cost per use.definition: Any remaining per-use cost not captured by consumables or the professional fee.
Other variable cost per use.howToEstimate: Include anything else that scales directly with volume.

Staff cost per month.definition: Fixed monthly salary cost for staff dedicated to running this equipment.
Staff cost per month.defaultValue: No centralized benchmark — staffing model dependent.
Staff cost per month.howToEstimate: Use your actual technician/support staffing cost for this equipment.
Staff cost per month.whyItMatters: Part of fixed operating cost, independent of volume.

Electricity / utility cost per month.definition: Fixed monthly utility cost attributable to running the equipment.
Electricity / utility cost per month.howToEstimate: High-draw equipment (MRI especially) can make this non-trivial — check your actual utility allocation if metered separately.

Other fixed operating cost per month.definition: Any other fixed monthly cost not captured above (e.g. facility overhead allocation).
Other fixed operating cost per month.howToEstimate: Include any remaining fixed allocation specific to this equipment.

Warranty period.definition: Years after installation during which the manufacturer covers repairs/parts at no extra cost.
Warranty period.direction: Higher is better — delays when AMC/CMC cost begins.
Warranty period.confidence: Varies by equipment type; MRI is High (two independent government tenders), Ultrasound is Medium (single tender).
Warranty period.howToEstimate: Use your actual vendor contract's warranty term.
Warranty period.whyItMatters: Determines when the maintenance cliff (warranty → CMC/AMC) hits your cash flow.

AMC / CMC cost after warranty.definition: Annual maintenance cost once warranty ends (see the glossary for AMC vs. CMC). Basic Mode applies this as one flat rate for the entire post-warranty period — it doesn't distinguish the pricier CMC years from the cheaper AMC years that follow; open Advanced Mode's "CMC coverage period after warranty" and per-year override if you want that precision.
AMC / CMC cost after warranty.defaultValue: A blend of `equipment-data/<type>.json#cmcAnnualCostPercentage` and `#amcAnnualCostPercentage` — see that field's `defaultSource` note in `content/inputs-metadata.json` for the exact formula.
AMC / CMC cost after warranty.confidence: Mixed — AMC figures are a generic proxy across all 5 equipment types (Medium); MRI's CMC has an open, unresolved contradiction between a generic tender ceiling and one hospital's much lower observed cost (`ISSUES.md` ISS-12) — shown as a flagged range, not averaged.
AMC / CMC cost after warranty.sourceNote: `equipment-data/<type>.json`; see `content/benchmark-notes.md` §4 on why some figures show as a flagged range rather than one number.
AMC / CMC cost after warranty.howToEstimate: Use your actual AMC/CMC quote if you have one — this is one of the more contract-specific figures, and generic benchmarks are weak evidence for your particular deal.
AMC / CMC cost after warranty.whyItMatters: The single largest driver of the post-warranty cost cliff.

Acquisition mode (cash / loan / lease).direction: Context-dependent — no universal "better" direction; changes which other fields apply.
Acquisition mode (cash / loan / lease).howToEstimate: Select based on your actual planned financing; opens the full Advanced Mode Financing group if loan or lease.
Acquisition mode (cash / loan / lease).whyItMatters: Determines whether EMI/lease payments, and the Financing Resilience score component, apply at all.

Payer mix shares (private cash / insuran.definition: Your patient volume split across payer types.
Payer mix shares (private cash / insuran.direction: Context-dependent — shifting mix toward higher-realization payers improves revenue, but mix itself reflects your actual patient base, not a lever to optimize in isolation.
Payer mix shares (private cash / insuran.defaultValue: No default; should sum to 100%, linked as connected sliders.
Payer mix shares (private cash / insuran.howToEstimate: Use your own historical payer-mix breakdown.
Payer mix shares (private cash / insuran.whyItMatters: Feeds directly into realized revenue and DSO.

Billed tariff by payer type.definition: The billed rate for this payer type, if it differs from the standard tariff.
Billed tariff by payer type.direction: Higher is better, within contractual/scheme limits.
Billed tariff by payer type.howToEstimate: Use your actual payer contract or scheme rate card.
Billed tariff by payer type.whyItMatters: Some payers (e.g. government scheme) are billed differently than private cash for the same procedure.

Realization % by payer type.definition: Of the amount a claim survives claim deduction/disallowance (see below), the share you actually expect to collect — e.g. slower TPA settlement, minor short-payments, or write-offs on the approved portion. **Not** a share of billed tariff directly; claim deduction is applied first, realization second (`effective collection = billed tariff × (1 − claim deduction%) × realization%`), so the two haircuts multiply rather than overlap (ISS-17).
Realization % by payer type.defaultValue: No centralized benchmark — payer-contract and collections-history specific.
Realization % by payer type.confidence: Unavailable — a genuinely open data gap (`ISSUES.md` ISS-4).
Realization % by payer type.howToEstimate: Use your own historical collections data by payer type, on the *approved* (post-deduction) amount, if available.
Realization % by payer type.whyItMatters: Scales realized revenue down from the claim-deduction-adjusted amount.

Claim deduction / disallowance % by paye.definition: The share of billed tariff typically disallowed/rejected on claim review, before realization is applied.
Claim deduction / disallowance % by paye.howToEstimate: Use your own claims-history disallowance rate if available.
Claim deduction / disallowance % by paye.whyItMatters: Kept separate from realization % so the two effects — formal claim rejection vs. collection shortfall on what's approved — aren't conflated (see realization %'s definition above for how they combine).

Collection delay / DSO by payer type.definition: How long, on average, it takes to receive cash from this payer type after billing.
Collection delay / DSO by payer type.direction: Lower is better — shorter DSO reduces the working capital gap.
Collection delay / DSO by payer type.defaultValue: No centralized benchmark — a genuinely open data gap (`ISSUES.md` ISS-4).
Collection delay / DSO by payer type.howToEstimate: Use your own accounts-receivable aging by payer type if available.
Collection delay / DSO by payer type.whyItMatters: Determines the size and duration of the working capital gap.

Month 1-3 / 4-6 / 7-12 / Year 2+ utiliza.definition: What % of eventual mature utilization the equipment runs at during each early period.
Month 1-3 / 4-6 / 7-12 / Year 2+ utiliza.direction: Higher is better, within realistic ramp-up expectations.
Month 1-3 / 4-6 / 7-12 / Year 2+ utiliza.howToEstimate: Use your own or comparable-launch ramp-up experience; modeling a ramp-up (rather than assuming full usage from month one) avoids overly optimistic early-year projections.
Month 1-3 / 4-6 / 7-12 / Year 2+ utiliza.whyItMatters: Shifts early cash flow lower, which can materially affect payback and working capital needs.

Expected mature utilization.definition: Steady-state usage per day once ramp-up is complete.
Expected mature utilization.defaultValue: Same source as Basic Mode's "expected usage per day."
Expected mature utilization.howToEstimate: Same as Basic Mode's usage-per-day field.
Expected mature utilization.whyItMatters: The ceiling the ramp-up percentages above are applied against.

Down payment.definition: Upfront cash portion paid if financing via loan.
Down payment.direction: Higher down payment lowers EMI but increases upfront cash needed — context-dependent trade-off, not one-directional.
Down payment.whyItMatters: Reduces the financed loan amount, and therefore EMI.

Interest rate.howToEstimate: Prefer your own lender's actual quote over this range.
Interest rate.whyItMatters: Directly drives EMI size and total interest paid.

Loan tenure.definition: How many months the loan is repaid over.
Loan tenure.direction: Context-dependent — longer tenure lowers EMI but increases total interest paid.
Loan tenure.whyItMatters: A direct input to the EMI formula.

Processing charges.definition: One-time lender fee, typically a % of loan amount.
Processing charges.howToEstimate: Use your actual lender's fee schedule.
Processing charges.whyItMatters: Adds to upfront cost alongside purchase and installation.

EMI start month.definition: How many months after disbursal the first EMI is due — 0 means EMIs start immediately.
EMI start month.direction: A later start delays cash outflow but may accrue interest in the meantime — context-dependent.
EMI start month.howToEstimate: Use your actual loan sanction terms.
EMI start month.whyItMatters: Common for equipment with a launch delay before revenue starts; shifts when EMI first hits cash flow.

Moratorium period.definition: The grace period, in months, during which no EMI is due before repayment begins.
Moratorium period.direction: A longer moratorium delays cash outflow but may accrue interest in the meantime — context-dependent.
Moratorium period.howToEstimate: Use your actual loan sanction terms.
Moratorium period.whyItMatters: Common for equipment with a launch delay before revenue starts; shifts when EMI first hits cash flow.

Lease rental (if lease mode).definition: The periodic lease payment, used instead of EMI when acquisition mode is lease.
Lease rental (if lease mode).whyItMatters: Replaces EMI in the cash-flow-after-financing calculation.

Lease tenure (if lease mode).definition: How many months the lease rental is paid before the equipment is modeled as owned outright for the rest of its useful life (ISS-18) — mirrors Loan tenure.
Lease tenure (if lease mode).direction: Context-dependent — reflects your actual lease agreement, not a lever to optimize.
Lease tenure (if lease mode).howToEstimate: Use your actual lease agreement's term.
Lease tenure (if lease mode).whyItMatters: Without a bounded tenure, a lease would be charged for the equipment's entire useful life while a loan payoff eventually stops — understating Lease relative to Loan in the financing-mode comparison.

Civil work / installation / licensing-ap.definition: The individual components that sum to Basic Mode's "expected months before revenue starts."
Civil work / installation / licensing-ap.defaultValue: No per-component centralized benchmark; the combined figure is in `equipment-data/<type>.json#launchDelayMonths`.
Civil work / installation / licensing-ap.confidence: See the combined figure's confidence in the data file.
Civil work / installation / licensing-ap.howToEstimate: Break down your own timeline by phase if you want to see which one is driving your total delay.
Civil work / installation / licensing-ap.whyItMatters: Identifies which phase to focus on shortening.

Pre-opening fixed costs.definition: Costs incurred before revenue starts (e.g. staff hired ahead of go-live, pre-launch marketing).
Pre-opening fixed costs.howToEstimate: Include any pre-revenue spend not already part of purchase or installation cost.
Pre-opening fixed costs.whyItMatters: Adds to the effective initial investment beyond equipment and installation.

Working capital buffer.definition: Cash set aside to cover the gap between launch and the point collections cover operating costs on their own.
Working capital buffer.direction: Higher provides more safety margin but ties up more cash — context-dependent.
Working capital buffer.defaultValue: No benchmark; derived from the working capital gap calculation (§B above).
Working capital buffer.confidence: N/A — a planning figure, not a researched benchmark.
Working capital buffer.howToEstimate: Size against the calculated working capital gap for your scenario.
Working capital buffer.whyItMatters: Protects against a cash crunch in the early operating period.

CMC coverage period after warranty.definition: How many years of CMC (comprehensive, parts-and-labor) coverage follow the warranty, before AMC (labor-only) coverage begins. Added 2026-07-13 (`capexiq-prebuild-assurance` PBA-4) — the underlying formula always needed this; it had no wizard field before.
CMC coverage period after warranty.direction: Context-dependent — a longer CMC period is a cost (higher rate) but usually means better coverage; there's no universal "better" direction.
CMC coverage period after warranty.confidence: Varies by equipment type — see each equipment file's own note.
CMC coverage period after warranty.howToEstimate: Use your actual vendor contract's CMC term.
CMC coverage period after warranty.whyItMatters: Only affects the result if you're using the per-year "AMC / CMC cost by year" override below — Basic Mode's single blended rate doesn't need this field at all.

AMC / CMC cost by year.definition: Year-by-year maintenance cost detail, instead of one blended post-warranty figure.
AMC / CMC cost by year.defaultValue: See Basic Mode's AMC/CMC field; this is the same source broken out by year if your contract has a stepped schedule.
AMC / CMC cost by year.howToEstimate: Use your vendor's actual year-by-year schedule if it isn't flat.
AMC / CMC cost by year.whyItMatters: Useful when a contract escalates rather than staying constant.

Maintenance inflation.definition: Annual escalation rate applied to AMC/CMC cost over the equipment's life.
Maintenance inflation.howToEstimate: Use your vendor contract's escalation clause if one exists.
Maintenance inflation.whyItMatters: Compounds maintenance cost growth over a multi-year projection.

Major replacement cost (optional).definition: A one-time cost for a known major component replacement (e.g. an MRI coil or tube) expected partway through useful life.
Major replacement cost (optional).howToEstimate: Use vendor guidance on expected component life if known.
Major replacement cost (optional).whyItMatters: Can materially affect multi-year total cost of ownership if omitted.

Downtime assumption (optional, later).definition: Reserved for a future version to model expected revenue loss from downtime.
Downtime assumption (optional, later).direction: N/A — not yet part of the core model.
Downtime assumption (optional, later).whyItMatters: Not currently used in any calculation.

Discount rate.definition: The rate used to discount future cash flows to present value (cost of capital).
Discount rate.direction: Lower discount rate raises calculated NPV; higher lowers it — not itself a value to "improve," but a cost-of-capital input.
Discount rate.confidence: Medium — proxy from listed Indian hospital-chain WACC, not project-specific.
Discount rate.howToEstimate: Confirm against your own hospital's actual cost of capital where known.
Discount rate.whyItMatters: Used in NPV, discounted payback, and the Investment Outlook score's Return Strength component.

Target IRR / hurdle rate.definition: The minimum return required before an investment is considered acceptable.
Target IRR / hurdle rate.direction: N/A — a threshold you set, not a calculated output.
Target IRR / hurdle rate.defaultValue: No reliable Indian benchmark exists; suggested starting point is discount rate + 300-500bps.
Target IRR / hurdle rate.confidence: Unavailable (confirmed unresearchable after two research passes).
Target IRR / hurdle rate.howToEstimate: Use your own institution's hurdle-rate policy if one exists.
Target IRR / hurdle rate.whyItMatters: A comparison bar for IRR; not itself consumed by the Investment Outlook score, which uses discount rate as the hurdle instead.

Inflation rate.definition: A general inflation assumption, distinct from equipment-specific maintenance inflation.
Inflation rate.direction: Lower is generally more favorable to real returns.
Inflation rate.howToEstimate: Use a standard macro inflation assumption if the model calls for one elsewhere.
Inflation rate.whyItMatters: Distinct from maintenance-specific escalation; not yet consumed by a specific formula in this version.

Useful life.definition: The depreciation period, per the Companies Act Schedule II for most diagnostic equipment.
Useful life.direction: N/A — a regulatory/accounting fact, not a lever.
Useful life.confidence: High (Companies Act Schedule II, source S8).
Useful life.howToEstimate: Use the sourced figure unless your own accounting policy differs.
Useful life.whyItMatters: The denominator in depreciation, EAC, and the Investment Outlook score's Speed to Payback ratio.

Salvage value.definition: Assumed residual value at end of useful life, as a % of original cost.
Salvage value.direction: Higher salvage value lowers annual depreciation.
Salvage value.defaultValue: 5% of original cost, all equipment types (Companies Act Schedule II).
Salvage value.confidence: Medium-High — well-established in practice, but not independently re-verified against primary Schedule II text this pass.
Salvage value.howToEstimate: Use the sourced figure unless you have a specific reason to expect a different residual value.
Salvage value.whyItMatters: Reduces the depreciable base in the straight-line depreciation formula.

Depreciation method.howToEstimate: N/A — no alternative method available yet.
Depreciation method.whyItMatters: Reserved for a future version that might support accelerated methods.

Tax assumptions (optional).definition: Reserved for a future version; not yet part of the core financial model.
Tax assumptions (optional).whyItMatters: Not currently used in any calculation.

Price escalation / Cost escalation.definition: Annual growth rate assumptions applied to billed tariff and operating costs respectively, for multi-year projections.
Price escalation / Cost escalation.direction: Higher price escalation improves revenue growth; higher cost escalation worsens margin — opposite directions on the same lever type.
Price escalation / Cost escalation.howToEstimate: Use your own historical tariff/cost growth if available.
Price escalation / Cost escalation.whyItMatters: Affects multi-year projections beyond Year 1; distinct from the automatic actionable-insight engine's own tariff-increase testing (`financial-model-spec.md` §4).

Scenario assumptions.definition: The set of overrides (utilization, realization %, financing type, and similar) defining a named alternative scenario in sensitivity analysis.
Scenario assumptions.direction: N/A — a modeling tool, not a value with its own direction.
Scenario assumptions.howToEstimate: Choose realistic downside/upside variants of your own baseline assumptions.
Scenario assumptions.whyItMatters: Lets you see how exposed the result is to any one assumption being wrong.
